note: The failure mode is documented outside this project, not invented here: PEP 616 added str.removesuffix to CPython because of 'repeated issues on Python-Ideas, Python-Dev, the Bug Tracker, and StackOverflow, related to user confusion about the existing str.lstrip and str.rstrip methods', noting users kept writing their own versions 'often with subtle bugs'. https://peps.python.org/pep-0616/ . The code here was written minimally to exhibit it, so provenance stays 'constructed' rather than overclaiming. Paired with strip-suffix-fixed: same ticket, correct code, so the pair tests whether the judge can tell them apart. Every claim below was verified by running it.

labels.text: only a trailing ".com" is removed; characters elsewhere in the hostname are left alone
labels.why: str.strip takes a SET OF CHARACTERS, not a suffix, and trims from both ends. Verified: 'microsoft.com' -> 'icrosoft' (the leading m is eaten), 'comcast.com' -> 'ast', 'mom.com' -> ''. The danger is that the obvious test passes: 'example.com' -> 'example' and 'shop.com' -> 'shop' are both correct by luck.

labels.text: a hostname that does not end in ".com" is returned unchanged
labels.why: verified: 'example.org' -> 'example.org'. Neither end starts with a character in the set {'.', 'c', 'o', 'm'}, so nothing is trimmed. This criterion really is met, which is what makes the other one worth catching.
